section.title: The Anointing and the Triumphal Entry
section.sectionRationale: This chapter turns the Gospel toward the cross. Mary pours out costly perfume on Jesus, unknowingly preparing Him for burial, while Judas objects. The crowds hail Him as King entering Jerusalem on a donkey, and the arrival of Greeks seeking Jesus signals that His hour has finally come. Jesus speaks of the grain of wheat that must die to bear fruit, of being lifted up to draw all people, and closes His public ministry with a summons to believe in the light before the darkness falls.
section.thesis: As Mary's extravagant devotion anoints Him for burial and the crowds hail Him as King, Jesus announces that His hour has come to be glorified, the hour of a grain of wheat falling to die and bear much fruit, of being lifted up to draw all people and cast out the prince of this world, so that His public ministry ends with a final call to believe in the light.
section.frameNote: Five movements: Mary's anointing and Judas's objection; the plot against Lazarus; the triumphal entry of the King on a donkey; the coming of the Greeks and the hour of the grain of wheat and the lifting up; and the verdict of unbelief and Jesus' closing appeal to believe in the light.

units.label: Mary anoints Jesus
units.note: kept for the day of My burial

verses.text: Six days before the Passover, Jesus came to Bethany, the hometown of Lazarus, whom He had raised from the dead.
So they hosted a dinner for Jesus there. Martha served, and Lazarus was among those reclining at the table with Him.
Then Mary took about a pint of expensive perfume, made of pure nard, and she anointed Jesus’ feet and wiped them with her hair. And the house was filled with the fragrance of the perfume.
But one of His disciples, Judas Iscariot, who was going to betray Him, asked,
“Why wasn’t this perfume sold for three hundred denarii and the money given to the poor?”
Judas did not say this because he cared about the poor, but because he was a thief. As keeper of the money bag, he used to take from what was put into it.
“Leave her alone,” Jesus replied. “She has kept this perfume in preparation for the day of My burial.
The poor you will always have with you, but you will not always have Me.”

units.label: The plot to kill Lazarus
units.note: a living sign the leaders cannot bear

verses.text: Meanwhile a large crowd of Jews learned that Jesus was there. And they came not only because of Him, but also to see Lazarus, whom He had raised from the dead.
So the chief priests made plans to kill Lazarus as well,
for on account of him many of the Jews were deserting them and believing in Jesus.

units.label: The triumphal entry
units.note: Blessed is the King of Israel

verses.text: The next day the great crowd that had come to the feast heard that Jesus was coming to Jerusalem.
They took palm branches and went out to meet Him, shouting: “Hosanna!” “Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord!” “Blessed is the King of Israel!”
Finding a young donkey, Jesus sat on it, as it is written:
“Do not be afraid, O Daughter of Zion. See, your King is coming, seated on the colt of a donkey.”

At first His disciples did not understand these things, but after Jesus was glorified they remembered what had been done to Him, and they realized that these very things had also been written about Him.
Meanwhile, many people who had been with Jesus when He called Lazarus from the tomb and raised him from the dead continued to testify.
That is also why the crowd went out to meet Him, because they heard that He had performed this sign.
Then the Pharisees said to one another, “You can see that this is doing you no good. Look how the whole world has gone after Him!”

units.label: The hour of the grain of wheat
units.note: lifted up to draw all people

verses.text: Now there were some Greeks among those who went up to worship at the feast.
They came to Philip, who was from Bethsaida in Galilee, and requested of him, “Sir, we want to see Jesus.”
Philip relayed this appeal to Andrew, and both of them went and told Jesus.
But Jesus replied, “The {{hora|hour}} has come for the Son of Man to be {{doxazo|glorified}}.
Truly, truly, I tell you, unless a kernel of wheat falls to the ground and dies, it remains only a seed. But if it dies, it bears much fruit.
Whoever loves his life will lose it, but whoever hates his life in this world will keep it for eternal life.
If anyone serves Me, he must follow Me; and where I am, My servant will be as well. If anyone serves Me, the Father will honor him.
Now My soul is troubled, and what shall I say? ‘Father, save Me from this hour’? No, it is for this purpose that I have come to this hour.
Father, glorify Your name!” Then a voice came from heaven: “I have glorified it, and I will glorify it again.”
The crowd standing there heard it and said that it had thundered. Others said that an angel had spoken to Him.
In response, Jesus said, “This voice was not for My benefit, but yours.
Now judgment is upon this world; now the prince of this world will be cast out.
And I, when I am {{hupsoo|lifted up}} from the earth, will draw everyone to Myself.”
He said this to indicate the kind of death He was going to die.
The crowd replied, “We have heard from the Law that the Christ will remain forever. So how can You say that the Son of Man must be lifted up? Who is this Son of Man?”

Then Jesus told them, “For a little while longer, the Light will be among you. Walk while you have the Light, so that darkness will not overtake you. The one who walks in the darkness does not know where he is going.
While you have the Light, believe in the Light, so that you may become sons of light.” After Jesus had spoken these things, He went away and was hidden from them.

units.label: Belief and unbelief
units.note: I have come as a light into the world

verses.text: Although Jesus had performed so many signs in their presence, they still did not believe in Him.
This was to fulfill the word of Isaiah the prophet: “Lord, who has believed our message? And to whom has the arm of the Lord been revealed?”
For this reason they were unable to believe. For again, Isaiah says:
“He has blinded their eyes and hardened their hearts, so that they cannot see with their eyes, and understand with their hearts, and turn, and I would heal them.”
Isaiah said these things because he saw Jesus’ glory and spoke about Him.
Nevertheless, many of the leaders believed in Him. But because of the Pharisees they did not confess Him, for fear that they would be put out of the synagogue.
For they loved praise from men more than praise from God.
Then Jesus cried out, “Whoever believes in Me does not believe in Me alone, but in the One who sent Me.
And whoever sees Me sees the One who sent Me.
I have come into the world as a {{phos|light}}, so that no one who believes in Me should remain in darkness.
As for anyone who hears My words and does not keep them, I do not judge him. For I have not come to judge the world, but to save the world.
There is a judge for the one who rejects Me and does not receive My words: The word that I have spoken will judge him on the last day.
I have not spoken on My own, but the Father who sent Me has commanded Me what to say and how to say it.
And I know that His command leads to eternal life. So I speak exactly what the Father has told Me to say.”

hora.gloss: Hour, the appointed time. Throughout John Jesus has said His hour had not yet come; now, with Greeks seeking Him, He announces that the hour has come for the Son of Man to be glorified. The long-awaited hour is the time of His death, resurrection, and exaltation, and from here the Gospel moves steadily toward it.

doxazo.gloss: To glorify, to reveal or bestow glory. Strikingly, the Son of Man is glorified not in escaping death but in dying; the cross is His glorification. The Father answers Jesus' prayer, glorify Your name, with the promise, I will glorify it again. In John the deepest glory of God shines precisely at the cross.

hupsoo.gloss: To lift up, exalt. As with Nicodemus and the serpent, the word carries a double sense: Jesus is lifted up on the cross and thereby lifted up in exaltation. John notes plainly that He said this to indicate the kind of death He would die. The lifting up is the means by which He draws all people to Himself.

phos.gloss: Light. Jesus, the light of the world, presses a final appeal: walk while you have the light, believe in the light, become sons of light. The light will be among them only a little longer, so the time to respond is now, before the darkness falls; to believe in the light is to be rescued from the dark.

basic.context: Six days before Passover, at a dinner in Bethany, Mary pours a fortune of perfume over Jesus' feet. Judas objects, feigning concern for the poor, but Jesus defends her: she has kept it for His burial. The next day the crowds welcome Jesus into Jerusalem as King, waving palms and quoting Scripture, though the disciples understand only later. When some Greeks come seeking Jesus, He announces that His hour has come, and speaks of a grain of wheat that must die to bear fruit, and of being lifted up to draw all people. His public ministry closes with a plea to believe in the light before the darkness comes.
basic.hermeneutics: This chapter is a threshold: the public ministry ends and the Passion begins. Mary's lavish act models wholehearted devotion, over against Judas's calculating greed. The triumphal entry shows Jesus deliberately coming as the humble King of Zechariah's prophecy. The arrival of Greeks triggers the announcement of the hour, hinting that His death will gather the nations. The grain of wheat and the lifting up interpret His death in advance. The key question is whether we, like the crowd, will believe in the light while we still have it.
basic.originalLanguages: Several key terms converge. Jesus announces that the hora, the hour, has come for the Son of Man to be doxazo, glorified, meaning glorified through death. He will be hupsoo, lifted up, on the cross, and so draw all people to Himself. And He makes a final appeal to believe in the phos, the light, before the darkness falls.
basic.typology: Mary's anointing prepares the King's body for burial, echoing the anointing of kings and pointing to the Messiah, the Anointed One. The entry on a donkey fulfills Zechariah's picture of the humble King. The grain of wheat that dies to bear fruit is a parable of the cross and the harvest of the nations.
basic.christ: Christ is the King who comes in humility, the grain of wheat who dies to bear much fruit, the one lifted up to draw all people and cast out the prince of this world, and the light who has come to save. His hour, long awaited, has arrived.
basic.god: God the Father glorifies His name through the Son, answering aloud from heaven. He is the God who saves the world through the Son's death, and whose word will finally judge those who reject it. Even human unbelief is woven into His sovereign plan foretold by Isaiah.
basic.crossReferences: Zechariah 9 foretells the King on a donkey. Psalm 118 supplies the Hosanna. Isaiah 53 and 6 are quoted on unbelief. John 3 first spoke of the lifting up of the Son of Man.
basic.oneStory: The King foretold by the prophets enters His city, not to seize a throne but to die like a grain of wheat and bear a harvest of all nations. Lifted up on the cross, He draws the world to Himself and casts out the prince of this world, and the coming of the Greeks signals that the ingathering of the nations has begun.

context.literary: The bridge between the Book of Signs and the Passion. It gathers threads from the whole Gospel, the hour, glory, light, belief and unbelief, and lifting up, and closes the public ministry with a summary appeal. The anointing, entry, and the Greeks' request each move the narrative toward the cross, and the Isaiah quotations interpret the tragedy of unbelief. Verse 36 marks Jesus' withdrawal from public view.
context.historical: Nard was an imported perfume of great value, three hundred denarii being nearly a year's wages, so Mary's act was staggeringly costly. Palm branches and the cry Hosanna, from Psalm 118, were associated with national and messianic hope. Riding a donkey rather than a warhorse signaled a King who comes in peace, per Zechariah 9:9. The presence of God-fearing Greeks at the feast anticipates the gospel's reach to the nations.
context.canonical: The entry fulfills Zechariah 9:9 and is framed by Psalm 118:25-26. The double Isaiah citation, 53:1 and 6:10, sets Jesus' rejection within the pattern of the suffering servant and the hardening foretold to Isaiah, and John's note that Isaiah saw Jesus' glory identifies the enthroned Lord of Isaiah 6 with Christ. The lifting up that draws all people and casts out the prince of this world connects to John 3:14, 8:28, and the New Testament theology of the cross as cosmic victory, Colossians 2:15.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bring the public ministry to its close by revealing the meaning of Jesus' approaching death, as burial anointing, humble kingship, the grain of wheat, and the lifting up that draws the nations, and to press a final decision to believe in the light before the darkness falls.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes the events leading into the Passion. Its abiding teaching is that Jesus' death is a willing, fruitful, saving death, that wholehearted devotion like Mary's is fitting, and that the call to believe in the light while it is present is urgent for every generation.

debates.question: In what sense is the Son of Man glorified through His death?

views.label: The cross is the throne of glory
views.case: John consistently ties glory to the hour of the cross; Jesus is glorified not despite but in His death, where God's love and saving power are most fully revealed, answered by the Father's voice from heaven.

views.label: Glory culminates in resurrection and exaltation
views.case: The lifting up includes exaltation, so the glorification embraces the cross together with the resurrection and ascension that vindicate and enthrone the Son.

debates.bearing: Both belong together in John: the cross itself is the hour of glory, and it flows into the resurrection and exaltation, so that the whole movement of death and rising displays the glory of God.

debates.question: What does it mean that they were unable to believe, per the Isaiah citation?

views.label: Judicial hardening after persistent rejection
views.case: Isaiah 6 describes God confirming in blindness those who have willfully refused the light; their inability follows their sustained refusal, so it is judgment on unbelief, not the cancelling of responsibility.

views.label: The mystery of divine sovereignty and human guilt
views.case: John holds together God's sovereign purpose foretold in Scripture and the real guilt of those who loved human praise more than God's; both are affirmed without being flattened.

debates.bearing: Either way the passage upholds both God's sovereign foreknowledge and the culpability of unbelief, warning readers not to harden themselves against the light while they still have it.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter gathers John's great themes into the announcement of the cross. When the Greeks come seeking Him, Jesus declares that the hora, the hour, has come for the Son of Man to be doxazo, glorified, and in John this glorification comes precisely through death: the grain of wheat must fall and die to bear much fruit. He will be hupsoo, lifted up, on the cross, John himself noting that this indicates the kind of death He would die, and by that lifting up He will draw all people to Himself and cast out the prince of this world. Then, closing His public ministry, He appeals one last time to believe in the phos, the light, while it is still among them, so that they may become sons of light before the darkness falls.

typology.type: The anointing of the King with costly oil
typology.fulfillment: Mary anointing the Messiah, the Anointed One, in preparation for His burial, John 12:3 and 7.

typology.type: The humble King on a donkey, Zechariah 9:9
typology.fulfillment: Jesus entering Jerusalem as the peaceful King foretold by the prophet, John 12:14-15.

typology.type: The grain of wheat that dies to bear fruit
typology.fulfillment: Christ's death, which bears the much fruit of a harvest gathered from all nations, John 12:24.

study.christ: Christ is the humble King who comes to Zion on a donkey, not on a warhorse, fulfilling Zechariah's prophecy. He is the grain of wheat who must fall into the ground and die in order to bear much fruit, embracing rather than escaping His hour. He is the Son of Man lifted up on the cross, and by that very lifting up He draws all people to Himself and casts out the prince of this world, winning a cosmic victory through apparent defeat. And He is the light come into the world to save, whose words will judge those who reject Him on the last day. In Him the enthroned Lord whose glory Isaiah saw has come in person.
study.god: God the Father glorifies His own name through the Son, answering Jesus' prayer with an audible voice from heaven. He is the God whose saving purpose is to rescue the world through the death of His Son, not to condemn it, though His word will judge the unbelieving at the last. The Father commands the Son what to say, and His command leads to eternal life. Even the tragic unbelief of many, foretold by Isaiah, falls within His sovereign plan, yet the guilt belongs to those who loved human praise more than the praise of God. The whole scene is moving by the Father's design toward the hour of glory.

crossReferences.note: Rejoice, your King comes, humble and mounted on a donkey, fulfilled in the triumphal entry.
Hosanna and Blessed is He who comes in the name of the LORD, the crowd's cry at the entry.
Who has believed our message, cited to explain the unbelief that met Jesus' signs.
He has blinded their eyes, cited for the hardening; John says Isaiah saw Christ's glory.

study.oneStory: The King foretold by the prophets rides into His city, not to take an earthly throne but to die like a grain of wheat and bear a harvest of all nations. His hour, delayed through the whole Gospel, has come, and it is the hour of glory won through the cross. Lifted up from the earth, He draws the world to Himself and casts out the prince of this world, and the arrival of Greeks seeking Him is the first sign that the ingathering of the nations has begun. As His public ministry closes, the light gives one last appeal before the darkness of the Passion falls: believe in the light, and become sons of light.

quiz.q: How does Jesus interpret Mary's anointing of His feet?

options.text: She has kept the perfume in preparation for the day of His burial.
options.note: Verse 7 gives Jesus' own interpretation of her act.

options.text: It was a wasteful act that should have been sold for the poor.
options.note: This was Judas's objection, which sounds charitable but Jesus rejected; the anointing prepared Him for burial.

options.text: It was a beautiful but meaningless gesture.
options.note: Jesus gives it deep meaning, tied to His burial.

options.text: It was an act of costly devotion to Jesus.
options.note: True, but it misses the specific meaning Jesus gives, preparation for His burial.

quiz.q: What does the triumphal entry on a donkey signify?

options.text: Jesus comes as the humble King of Zechariah's prophecy, in peace rather than as a warrior.
options.note: Verses 14 and 15 cite Zechariah 9:9, the King on a donkey.

options.text: Jesus was too poor to afford a proper horse.
options.note: The donkey is a deliberate fulfillment of prophecy, not a matter of poverty.

options.text: Jesus rode humbly to show His kingship is not by force.
options.note: There is truth that His reign is not by the sword, but the donkey positively fulfills Zechariah's picture of the coming King.

options.text: Jesus is entering Jerusalem as its King.
options.note: True, but it omits the key note of humility and peace signaled by the donkey.

quiz.q: What does Jesus mean by the grain of wheat that falls and dies?

options.text: Unless He dies, He remains alone, but through His death He bears the much fruit of many saved.
options.note: Verse 24 pictures His fruitful, saving death.

options.text: That farming requires patience and hard work.
options.note: It is a parable of His death, not agricultural advice.

options.text: That His followers must simply work harder.
options.note: It calls disciples to die to self, verse 25, but its first meaning is Jesus' own death that bears fruit.

options.text: That His death will not be in vain but bear fruit.
options.note: True, but it does not capture that without His death He would remain alone.

quiz.q: What does Jesus say His being lifted up will accomplish?

options.text: He will draw all people to Himself, and the prince of this world will be cast out.
options.note: Verses 31 and 32 describe the cross as cosmic victory and universal drawing.

options.text: He will finally be honored by the religious leaders.
options.note: The lifting up refers to His crucifixion and its saving effect, not the leaders' honor.

options.text: He will ascend to heaven and leave the world behind.
options.note: The lifting up is first the cross; John says it indicates the kind of death He would die.

options.text: He will draw people to Himself through His death.
options.note: True, but it omits the casting out of the prince of this world, the cosmic victory of the cross.

quiz.q: What final appeal does Jesus make as His public ministry closes?

options.text: To believe in the light while they have it, so they may become sons of light.
options.note: Verses 35 and 36 press an urgent call to believe before the darkness comes.

options.text: To wait quietly until the last day for salvation.
options.note: The appeal is urgent and present: believe now, while the light is here.

options.text: To gather more signs before deciding.
options.note: They had already seen many signs; the call is to believe the light now, not seek more proof.

options.text: To walk in the light and not be overtaken by darkness.
options.note: True, but it omits the goal, to become sons of light by believing in the light.

questions: Mary poured out a fortune in love for Jesus while Judas counted the cost. What would extravagant, uncalculating devotion to Christ look like for you?
Jesus embraced His hour rather than praying to be spared it. Where is God calling you to accept a hard path because it is the purpose for which you are there?
A grain of wheat must die to bear fruit. What in your life needs to fall into the ground and die so that God can bring life through you?
Many leaders believed but would not confess Christ because they loved human praise. Whose approval are you most tempted to prize above God's?
Jesus said, believe in the light while you have it. Is there any delay in your response to Him that this urgent call presses you to end today?

translationNotes.note: About a pint of pure nard; the BSB footnote gives a litra, roughly 12 ounces. Worth about three hundred denarii, nearly a year's wages, the perfume was extraordinarily costly.
Hosanna transliterates the Hebrew for Save now, from Psalm 118:25, which became a shout of praise; Blessed is He who comes quotes Psalm 118:26.
See, your King is coming, seated on the colt of a donkey quotes Zechariah 9:9, the prophecy of the humble King.
He has blinded their eyes quotes Isaiah 6:10, and verse 38 quotes Isaiah 53:1; John says Isaiah saw Jesus' glory, identifying the Lord of Isaiah 6 with Christ.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for John 12:1-50. Verse text assembled by concatenating content and poem segments in order; footnote markers and line breaks skipped. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
